coredata.dc:title: Real-time Lithium-ion battery state of health evaluation based on discrete wavelet transform: The effect of operating temperature
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Pelosi, D.
Gallorini, F.
Ottaviano, P.A.
Barelli, L.
coredata.dc:description: Li-ion batteries (LIBs), thanks to high efficiencies and energy density, represent the mainstream technology to replace traditional internal combustion vehicles with electric ones. However, LIBs state of health (SoH) should be investigated to avoid fast degradation due to fast-charging, electrical, mechanical and thermal factors. Therefore, SoH prediction and monitoring for battery electric vehicles is necessary for extending LIB lifespan and avoiding failures. In this paper, an accurate real-time SoH prediction and monitoring method, based on discrete wavelet (DWT) analysis, is investigated through an extensive experimental campaign considering the effect of temperature variation. Specifically, moving from cycle aging performed on Li-ion NCR 18650 cells and applying two typical US test drive cycles at different SoHs, three different operating temperatures (i.e., 0 °C, 20 °C and 30 °C) were investigated. Applying DWT on the gathered LIB voltage profiles, it is demonstrated that temperature effect on the implemented method is easily recognizable from the one of cycle aging. Moreover, suitable linearized functions are identified to refer DWT outcomes assessed at the operative temperature to a reference temperature, at which a suitable equation is previously identified to assess capacity fading. Due to its general validity the method can be extended to stationary applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Discrete wavelet transform
Temperature effect
Battery aging
State of health estimation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Testing procedure for capacity fading prediction
Effect of operating temperature on aging: experimental design

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Vehicle speed and battery current profile over the cycle time, in relation to the considered urban SC-03 Test cycle (Fig. 1a and b) and extra urban US06 Test cycle (Fig. 1c and d).
Schematic view of the experimental test rig.

Part of the experimental campaign (30 full aging cycles performed at constant current followed by full charge, extra urban profile, full charge and urban profile application) carried out on Panasonic NCR 18650 cell.
a) Detailed and b) Approximation coefficients values gathered for the cycled NCR cell.
Measured and predicted capacity fade for the cycled NCR cell.
Voltage trends during the application of the extra urban drive test profile at the three considered operating temperatures on the fresh Panasonic NCR 18650 cell.

Trends of a)
V
D n
and b)
V A
vs. temperature. Dotted lines in the figures a) and b) represent the linearized functions of
V
D n
and
V A
, to be implemented in the N1 and N2 equations. R2 values of linearized functions are illustrated in figures a) and b) for
V
D n
and
V A
.
Details and approximation coefficients at different temperatures and aging levels.

V

and
V A
indexes.

head.ce:title: Real-time Lithium-ion battery state of health evaluation based on discrete wavelet transform: The effect of operating temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering, University of Perugia, Via G. Duranti 93, 06125, Perugia, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Engineering, University of Perugia, Via G. Duranti 93, 06125 Perugia, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: VGA srl, Via dell’Innovazione SNC, 06053, Deruta, PG, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: VGA srl, Via dell’Innovazione SNC, 06053 Deruta (PG), Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Li-ion batteries (LIBs), thanks to high efficiencies and energy density, represent the mainstream technology to replace traditional internal combustion vehicles with electric ones. However, LIBs state of health (SoH) should be investigated to avoid fast degradation due to fast-charging, electrical, mechanical and thermal factors. Therefore, SoH prediction and monitoring for battery electric vehicles is necessary for extending LIB lifespan and avoiding failures. In this paper, an accurate real-time SoH prediction and monitoring method, based on discrete wavelet (DWT) analysis, is investigated through an extensive experimental campaign considering the effect of temperature variation. Specifically, moving from cycle aging performed on Li-ion NCR 18650 cells and applying two typical US test drive cycles at different SoHs, three different operating temperatures (i.e., 0 °C, 20 °C and 30 °C) were investigated. Applying DWT on the gathered LIB voltage profiles, it is demonstrated that temperature effect on the implemented method is easily recognizable from the one of cycle aging. Moreover, suitable linearized functions are identified to refer DWT outcomes assessed at the operative temperature to a reference temperature, at which a suitable equation is previously identified to assess capacity fading. Due to its general validity the method can be extended to stationary applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: To face with global warming, fossil fuel shortage and pollution, countries all over the world are replacing traditional internal combustion vehicles with Electric Vehicles (EVs). Due to battery fast development in the recent years, transportation electrification is getting a global consensus [1–5]. To date, Li-ion Batteries (LIBs) represent the mainstream technology to be used as on-board energy carrier to power electric vehicles, thanks to features as high efficiency and energy density, fast response, flexibility, scalability, excellent stability, low self-discharge and prolonged life cycle [6,7]. However, safety and reliability concerns must be addressed yet, being of utmost importance to the end-users [8]. To avoid LIBs fast degradation, fast charging, as well as electrical, mechanical and thermal factors, must be taken into account.

In this context, temperature plays a key role in charging/discharging processes and in power consumption. Indeed, temperature effects on battery aging is of paramount importance in transportation since it cannot be easily managed as in stationary applications. For instance, at temperatures below 0 °C, charging and discharging are impaired due to internal resistance increase; thus, the maximum and minimum voltages is reached earlier [9]. Instead, at higher temperatures, the charging and discharging performance generally remain good as the internal resistance decreases, but battery degradation and self-discharge accelerate due to higher chemical activity. Therefore, LIBs degradation depends not only on mechanical and electrical factors, as manufacturing inconsistency, charging and discharging C-rates, but also on the operating range of temperatures, resulting in a capacity fade that could rapidly decrease battery State of Health (SoH) [10,11].

Thus, an accurate on-line SoH prediction is essential to guarantee safety and reliability of a Li-ion battery. SoH estimation methods can be classified in: i) model-based and ii) data-driven methods [12]. Several methodologies have been proposed in literature for LIB SoH estimation and Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction; as example, Kalman filters, machine learning, neural networks, or fuzzy logic are used to calculate the degradation parameters of the Li-ion battery, as illustrated in Refs. [13–19]. Nevertheless, model-based methods require great computation efforts and a wide dataset for an accurate and reliable SoH prediction which are not easily implementable in Battery Management System (BMS) and on-board diagnosis (OBD) systems [20]. Among data-driven methods, discrete wavelet transform (DWT) can be an effective, easy-to-implement and computationally light solution when directly applied to the measured voltage profile [21–25]. DWT is known as a powerful tool allowing the extraction of electrochemical features from signals with a broad frequency range through multi-resolution analysis.

In this context, available research works implementing DWT technique do not consider the temperature effect on LIB aging since its behavior on battery degradation is still challenging [26,27]. Nevertheless, temperature can exert the greatest impact compared to other external factors [28].

To fill this gap, the present paper aims to investigate temperature effect on LIBs by means of DWT analysis. DWT is applied to different BEVs drive cycles to predict the effect of the operating temperature variation on battery SoH. Specifically, DWT is used to process the measured battery voltage and, subsequently, estimate the SoH. Such innovative approach allows an online accurate and easy-of-implementation LIB SoH prediction and monitoring.

To do that, firstly an experimental campaign of cycle aging performed on NCR 18650 cells was realized, applying two typical test drive drives (i.e., an urban and extra urban drive cycle) to the considered cells at different SoH and at fixed temperature of 20 °C. Subsequently, experimental tests were carried out at different temperatures demonstrating that temperature effect on DWT results prevails with respect to cycle aging effect. Thus, temperature effect can beeasily distinguished from cycle aging effect. Temperature variation can be implemented through linear functions. Once DWT coefficients are assessed based on voltage measurements at a certain operating temperature, such functions can be used to determine DWT details and approximation coefficients at the reference temperature (i.e., 20 °C). These coefficients are then implemented in the capacity fading function to compute the actual battery SoH.

At the best of authors’ knowledge, a model-free method based on DWT analysis to predict and monitoring battery SoH taking into account temperature variation has not been presented in scientific literature yet. Specifically, such method is able to predict LIB aging during BEV driving and at different operating temperatures within 0–30 °C range. Moreover, the easy-of-implementation of DWT on on-board diagnosis (OBD) systems allows to provide information in a continuous way to reduce battery degradation over the time. The high innovative character of the obtained findings has been also reported in a patent application by authors [29].

The paper is organized as follows: Section 2 describes the DWT theory and the chosen test drive cycles. Section 3 illustrates the experimental test rig and the preliminary tests to find the capacity fading function. The design of the experimental activity at different temperature is described in Section 4, while the main results of the study are discussed in Section 5.

The registered voltage at LIB terminals is a non-stationary signal; hence, the Fourier analysis is not effective because of the loss of the time information into the frequency domain passage [30]. To overcome such problem, wavelet analysis can be used to decompose a signal into shifted and scaled versions of the original wavelet [31]. The wavelet transform can be divided into continuous wavelet transform (CWT) and DWT. Anyway, DWT technique allows to reduce CWT complexity by means of decomposing the original signal in several resolutions [32]; therefore, it is widely employed for systems diagnosis [31]. Specifically, DWT separates the original signal into different time-frequency scales by means of digital filtering techniques. The signal passes through a series of high pass filters set at different cut-off frequencies to analyze the high frequencies; on the other hand, it goes through a series of low pass filters to explore the low frequencies. Hence, DWT is employed for multi resolution analysis (MRA), splitting the low frequency from the high frequency content of the considered signal [33]. Assuming n decomposition levels, the wavelet function
ξ
( t )
and the scaling function
ϑ
( t )
can be expressed as follows:
(1)
ξ
j , n
( t )
=
2
j
2
∑ n
w
j , n
ξ

2 j
t − n ]
(2)
ϑ
j , n
( t )
=
2
j
2
∑ n
u
j , n
ϑ
[
2 j
t − n ]
Where
w j
and
u j
represent the scaling coefficient and the wavelet at scale
j
, respectively.

According to Ref. [34], the MRA mathematical recursive representation can be defined as:
(3)

=
W
j + 1
⊕

Where:

-
V
j + 1
is the approximate version of the original signal at scale j +1;

-
W
j + 1
is the detailed version including all the signal transient phenomena at scale j +1;
- n is the decomposition level;

-
⊕
indicates the summation of the two decomposed signals.

Considering that the original signal
x j
( t )
at scale j is sampled at constant time intervals, e.g.,

(4)

Where

are the approximate and the detailed versions at scale j+1.

In practice, the acquired signal x(t), consisting of N samples where N = 2n, goes through a high-pass filter, determining the wavelet coefficients at the shortest scale j. Firstly, the procedure performs the signal down-sampling for j = 1. This produces an output signal with half length of the original. The corresponding wavelet coefficients vector
w D
1
has a length of 2n-1. Consequently, the original signal x(t) passes through the low-pass filter: this gives the approximated coefficients vector
u A
1
with length 2n-1. Then, the process is repeated since the condition n = j is reached.

Finally, Parseval’s theorem is applied to extract the energy distribution of the analyzed signal through DWT [34]. In Parseval’s theorem, if a discrete signal x(n) is the current that flows through a resistive element, the energy of the resistance is equal to the square sum of the spectrum coefficients (
a k
) of the Fourier transform in the frequency domain, as reported in eq. (8):
(8)
1 N
∑
n = 1
N
|
x
( n )
|
2
=
∑
n = 1
N
|
a k
|
2
Where N corresponds to the sampling period. Hence, the Parseval’s theorem representing the energy distribution of the signal in DWT is calculated by eq. (9):
(9)
1 N
∑ t
|
x
( t )
|
2
=
E a
+
E d
=
1
N j
∑ t

A

∑
j = 1
J

N j
∑ k

w
D
j , k

2
)
Where
E a
and
E d
are, respectively, the average power of the approximated version and the detailed version of the decomposed signal.

As mentioned in the Introduction, DWT analysis is applied to three different Li-ion cells. Such cells have been cycled repeating two different drive cycles selected according to Ref. [35]. In detail, the two test drive cycles correspond to:
-
the urban SC03 Test Drive, introduced by the Supplemental Federal Test Procedure (SFTP) to represent the engine load associated with use of air conditioning in vehicles certified over the FTP75 test cycle [36]. It corresponds to a mileage of 12.8 km at a maximum speed of 88.2 km/h and a duration of 596 s.
-
the extra urban SFTP-US06 Test Drive, representing the aggressive, high speed and/or high acceleration driving behavior, rapid speed fluctuations, and driving behavior following start-up [37]. It corresponds to a mileage of 12.8 km at a maximum speed of 129.2 km/h, with a duration of 596 s.

The vehicle speed over the time and the related battery current profiles of the BEV are illustrated in Fig. 1 in reference to the two implemented drive cycles. The considered vehicle for the BEV current calculation is a Renault ZOE, as detailed in Ref. [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing procedure for capacity fading prediction
ce:section.ce:para:
Aging tests to assess the operating temperature effect on LIBs have been realized on two Panasonic NCR 18650 Li-ion batteries, typically employed in transportation. Such cells can operate at maximum continuous dis-/charge currents of 2C and 0.7C respectively, with an expected lifetime of 300 cycles (cell SoH reaches the 80 % threshold value), according to manufacturer specification [38]. Cycle aging tests have been carried out for evaluating the actual cell degradation by means of coulomb counting method. The implemented procedure is detailed as follows:
1.
An initial battery capacity determination (BCD) has been performed at 0.5C-rate (corresponding to a current of about 1.7 A) to determine the capacity of the fresh cell. Specifically, BCD was carried out as a constant current (CC) followed by a constant voltage (CV), maintaining a constant voltage of 4.2V until the cut-off current of 65 mA was reached.
2.
Galvanostatic charge/discharge cycling have been repeated 30 times up to 150 cycles. Every 30 cycles, a full charge has been realized and the extra urban test drive cycle has been applied and repeated until the low voltage cut-off was reached. Subsequently, another full charge was performed, and the urban cycle repeated until the low voltage cut-off was reached, as evident in terms of the voltage evolution in Fig. 3 discussed in the following Section 3.2. This to evaluate cell behavior under cycling through DWT direct application on the registered voltage profile. Subsequently, BCD technique has been performed to assess battery capacity fading.
3.
After the cell reached 150 aging cycles, galvanostatic charge/discharge cycling at maximum C-rates have been repeated 75 times until the cell reaches its end-of-life (i.e., corresponding to 300 cycles, as indicated by battery manufacturer). Thus, as described above, extra urban and urban cycles were applied and repeated consequentially, followed by BCD.

A wider interval for BCD was chosen to be applied to the aged cell after 150 cycles since LIB capacity fade behavior tends to linearize after a rapid decrease at the initial stage, as indicated in Ref. [39]. Specifically, aging cycles were realized setting the voltage range within the safety limits (i.e., 2.5–4.2 V), and a 5-min resting time between charging and discharging processes was imposed. The charging C-rates for galvanostatic cycling were set to 0.3C and 0.7C (i.e., corresponding to a charge power of 22 kW (the typical power of a three-phase AC urban charging column) and 50 kW (a DC fast-charging column) respectively) for the two tested cells, while the discharge C-rate was fixed at 2C. The galvanostat/potentiostat SP-300, purchased from BioLogic®, and connected to the tested cells through by means of cable sensors, performed the aging experiments at a fixed temperature of 20 ± 1 °C. Fig. 2
depicts the experimental test rig.

The collected experimental voltage measurements relating to urban and extra urban profiles were post-processed via wavelet analysis in MATLAB. Fig. 3 depicts an exemplary part of the experimental test campaign, relating to 30 aging cycles and the subsequent application of the extra urban and urban profiles.

In detail, the original voltage signal was decomposed through DWT by using Daubechies basis of order three, in four decomposition levels [23]. It is demonstrated that all the detail coefficients increase while the approximation decreases in magnitude. This is a consequence of the aging effect on the cell under cycling. A trend reversal can be distinguished around 120/150 cycles, indicating the non-linear behavior of the Li-ion chemistry during cycling, as discussed in Refs. [40–42].

Fig. 4 depicts the detail coefficients (D1, D2, D3 and D4) and the approximation coefficient A determined by DWT, showing the percentage increase of the details and approximation decrease with respect to the registered previous values. It is noteworthy that, after a sudden increase of the details during the first 90 cycles, the behavior in terms of details and approximation stabilizes. Indeed, the reduction in terms of detail coefficients registered at 150 cycles underlines the battery capacity regeneration phenomena, which reduces the efficiency of model-based predictive methods for RUL estimation, as reported in Ref. [43]. This reflects the typical LIB capacity fading under cycling, emphasizing a greater decrease in the first cycles losing more than 10 % of the initial registered capacity and consequently a reduced slope until the expected end-of-life. Moreover, the capacity fading prediction based on DWT shows a good accuracy with no requirement of complex and elaborate models. This is simply obtained by assessing the percentage reduction of the details and approximation with respect to their previous values. Such relevant aspects make this method of easy implementation for BEV battery on-board diagnosis only by acquiring the voltage signal during the drive. Furthermore, the presented method requires no detailed measurement of the current and the knowledge of the battery capacity and initial SoC, with respect to coulombic counting methods, that can be only applied when the vehicle is stopped for charging.

By means of DWT analysis, a correlation between detail coefficients, approximation and number of cycles realized by the Li-ion battery was found to analytically describe the capacity fading, as reported in the following eq. (10):
(10)
C
f a d e
(
A ,
D
1 , 4
, N
)
=
A 0
A i
e
−
(
1 10
N i
A i
∑ i
∑
n = 1
4
D
n , i
∑
n = 1
4
D
n , 0
)
* 100
[ % ]
Where
A i
and
A 0
are the approximation coefficients related to i-th aging cycle and to the fresh battery, respectively.
N i
is the corresponding i-th number of cycles performed by the battery;
D
n , 0
and
D
n , i
represent the detail coefficients at n levels for the new battery and after
i
cycles of aging. Fig. 5 illustrates the measured and predicted (according to eq. (10)) percentage capacity fade over cycles. The RMSE value between predictions and real measurements is of 1.32 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of operating temperature on aging: experimental design
ce:section.ce:para: To assess temperature effect on cycle aging, three cycled cells were further investigated; specifically, a fresh cell and two aged cells (with 80 % and 77 % SoH, respectively) were considered. Cells were operated by discharging them, until the cut-off voltage was reached (i.e., at 2.5 V), applying US06 extra urban drive profile. Tests were performed for each cell at three different operating temperatures (i.e., 0, 20 and 30 °C). To this purpose every cell was tested in a climatic chamber. DWT analysis has been applied to a further cycled NCR cell to evaluate the variability of the wavelet analysis under the influence of temperature on voltage profiles.

In this section, the outcomes of the experimental campaign carried out on NCR 18650 cells are presented. As described in Section 4, the presented method aims to evaluate how temperature variation influences the capacity fading prediction and monitoring of BEV battery. Therefore, three NCR cells were tested each one at three different temperatures in a climatic chamber (i.e., 0, 20 and 30 °C). Specifically, a fresh cell and two aged cells were considered. Fig. 6 illustrates, as example, the voltage trends during the application of the extra urban drive test profile at the three considered operating temperatures. It is noteworthy as the internal resistance increase at low temperatures (as represented by blue line for 0 °C) leads to a lower operating voltage with higher amplitude variations.

Since LIB internal resistance is inversely proportional to temperature variation (i.e., a temperature reduction yields an internal resistance increase), a direct effect reflects on the amplitude of the battery voltage profiles. Therefore, such significant parameter was investigated for evaluating the effectiveness and efficiency of the implemented online model-free method under various operating conditions.

Specifically, the method is based on assessing details and approximation coefficients at different temperatures and aging conditions of the batteries, to understand the effect of temperature variation on such coefficients. The aim is to determine suitable relations to refer details and approximation coefficients, assessed at a certain operating temperature (Top), to the reference one (Tref), i.e. 20 °C at which the capacity fade function expressed by eq. (10) is referred. This because the operating conditions that occur in the implementation of this innovative method can vary compared to the Tref.
D
n , i
and
A i
values assessed at the ith cycle for the three NCR cells tested at 0, 20 and 30 °C are listed in Table 1 .

Data of Table 1 are analyzed to assess the extent of temperature impact on
D
n , i
and
A i
values, with respect to the one of cell aging on the same values. The datasets obtained, for each parameter (i.e., for each detail or approximation coefficient), grouping for temperature (considering in each resulting group data measured for all investigated degradation levels) and for SoH (including in each group data relative to all tested temperature values), are characterized assessing mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ). The resulting values are reported in Table 2 . It is highlighted that when data are grouped for temperature, the standard deviation assessed for the details coefficients significantly lowers. This indicates that temperature effect on those parameters prevails on the effect due to the operating temperature. Consequently, mean values of details and approximation coefficients (
D ‾
n
,
A ‾
) determined grouping
D
n , i
and
A i
values per temperature (see rows 1–3 of Table 2) are considered in the following.

Subsequently,
D ‾
n
and
A ‾
are used to define a correlation to be implemented in the capacity fading function calculated at Tref. To do that, the percentage deviations of
D ‾
n
and
A ‾
, with respect to the corresponding values assessed at 20 °C, are calculated according to eqs. (11) and (12). Such values are listed in Table 2, together with the mean and standard deviations of the detail coefficients and approximation.
(11)
V
D n
=
D ‾
n
T ref
−
D ‾
n
T o p
D ‾
n
T o p
[ % ]
(12)
V A
=
A ‾
T ref
−
A ‾
Top
A ‾
Top
[ % ]
Where
D ‾
n
T ref
and
D ‾
n
T o p
respectively represent the details coefficients at Tref and at the considered operating temperature
T op
, while
A ‾
T ref
and
A ‾
Top
are the approximation coefficients at Tref and at
T op
, respectively.
Surprisingly, VDn assumes the same value for all the details coefficients at parity of operating temperature.

Fig. 7 illustrates VDn and VA values varying the operating temperature. It is evident as, for an easy implementation in the electronic control unit of the vehicle, the trends can be linearized with a good accuracy. Consequently, on the basis of the mean values indicated in Table 2, the linearization procedure results in the N1 and N2 functions expressed by eqs. (13) and (14).
(13)
N 1
( T )
=
1 +
V
D n
= 1 + (
D ‾
n
T r e f
−
D ‾
n
T o p
) /
D ‾
n
T o p
(14)
N 2
( T )
=
1 +
V A
= 1 + (
A ‾
Tref
−
A ‾
T o p
) /
A ‾
T o p

It is highlighted as the trends can be determined through other more complex functions obtaining higher accuracy. Once voltage is measured at certain operating temperature and
D n
T o p
and
A Top
are consequently determined,
D
n
T ref
and
A
T ref
values can be computed by multiplying
D n
T o p
and
A Top
by N1 and N2 respectively. Finally,
D
n
T ref
and
A
T ref
can be implemented in the capacity fading function of eq. (10), whatever the operating temperature in the range 0–30 °C.

Therefore, since the temperature behavior on battery SoH estimation is yet to be well understood, this research work aims to demonstrate that the presented model-free method based on DWT analysis allows to distinguish temperature variation effect from cycle aging to predict and monitoring LIB SoH.

In this paper, a model-free real-time SoH prediction and monitoring method based on discrete wavelet (DWT) analysis is presented. Specifically, an extensive experimental campaign considering the effect of temperature variation was carried out. Moving from cycle aging performed on Li-ion NCR 18650 cells, three different operating temperatures (i.e., 0 °C, 20 °C and 30 °C) were investigated. Applying DWT on the gathered LIB voltage profiles, it is demonstrated that temperature effect can be separated from the one due to cycle aging. Moreover, DWT coefficients variation with temperature has been linearized by means of two functions. These functions allow to assess DWT coefficients at the reference temperature (i.e., fixed at 20 °C) once coefficients at the operating temperature are computed based on voltage measurements. Details and approximation DWT coefficients at the reference temperature are implemented in a capacity fade function suitably determined to predict battery SoH. The presented method allows an online easy implementation in on-board diagnosis systems for real-time SoH measurements during BEV driving. In addition, this method can also be extended to stationary applications, assessing in an accurate way the battery SoH, giving crucial information to the users to avoid battery fast degradation.

D. Pelosi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. F. Gallorini: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. P.A. Ottaviano: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. L. Barelli: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical Review of Life Cycle Assessment of Lithium-Ion Batteries for Electric Vehicles: A Lifespan Perspective,” eTransportation
